text: The efficient cleavage of C−C bonds and the complete oxidation of alcohols to CO₂ are critical hurdles in the development of high-performance fuel cells for renewable energy. Here, we report the synthesis of ultrathin (2.3 nm) core/shell Au/Pt nanowires (NWs) featuring stepped Pt clusters deposited along a stepped gold core. These specialized architectures serve as highly efficient catalysts for total alcohol oxidation. We demonstrate that catalytic performance is sensitive to the Au/Pt atomic ratio; specifically, Au₁.₀/Pt₀.₂ NWs—which possess the highest density of stepped Au/Pt sites (~75%)—exhibit an unprecedented mass activity of 196.9 A/mgPt (32.5 A/mgPt+Au) for ethanol electro-oxidation. This catalytic system also proves exceptionally effective for the oxidation of other primary alcohols, including methanol, n-propanol, and ethylene glycol. Using in situ X-ray absorption and infrared spectroscopies, we identified key reaction intermediates and characterized the catalyst structure, providing direct evidence that the synergy between low-coordinated Pt sites and the stepped Au surface is fundamental to the reaction mechanism. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations further corroborate that these undercoordinated Pt-Au surfaces significantly lower the energy barrier for C−C bond cleavage. This work highlights the design of stepped core/shell interfaces as a powerful strategy for enhancing electrochemical oxidation and provides a roadmap for the future development of highly efficient direct alcohol fuel cells.
